mappings.rationale: Section A.1 covers the ideal of paideia, Homeric education, and Hellenistic scholarship, all rooted in the classical tradition that defines humanistic scholarship.
Section A.1.a covers Homeric education and the ideal of the hero as the beginning of humanistic learning.
Section A.1.b covers the Sophists and Socrates and the turn to logos, the beginnings of rhetoric in the humanistic tradition.
Section A.1.c covers Plato and the Academy, the relation of theology to mythology, and mathematics in the service of philosophy.
Section A.1.d covers Aristotle and the Lyceum, the invention of logic, and the division and organization of the sciences.
Sections A.1.b-e cover the philosophical foundations of humanistic learning from the Sophists through Hellenistic scholarship.
Section A.1.b covers the beginnings of rhetoric, A.1.f covers the Roman ideal of training the orator, and A.1.g covers the conflict between rhetoric, philosophy, and science.
Section A.1.e covers the development of literary and textual criticism in Hellenistic scholarship, foundational to modern literary theory.
Section A.2.a covers the Christianization of pagan culture and reconciliation of classical humanism with Christian revelation, which Augustine exemplifies.
Section A.2.d covers the Scholastic method and the distinction of philosophy from sacred theology, central to Aquinas's contribution.
Section A.3.a covers the idea of Renaissance including the ideal of the classical, the rise of the vernacular, and the dignity of the individual.
The Renaissance (A.3.a) saw the flourishing of art as an expression of humanistic ideals about the classical and the dignity of the individual.
Section A.3.b covers humanism and the new learning as a key development in the history of humanistic scholarship.
Section A.3.d covers the separation of science from philosophy and the rise of empirical science, where Descartes's method was pivotal.
Section A.3.g covers the rise of the liberal arts college and graduate school, and B.2 covers the humanities as an educational program.
Section A.2.c covers the founding of universities, and A.3.g-i cover the development of the contemporary university and the profession of learning.
Philosophy is central to the humanities (B.1-3) and the history of learning, from Plato's Academy through the quarrel of ancients and moderns (A.3.e).
Section A.2 covers Christian learning in the Middle Ages including the codification of liberal arts, the trivium and quadrivium, and the Scholastic method.
Section A.5 covers the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship, its nature, methods, and relation to classical and Christian learning.
Section A.6 covers humanities and humanistic scholarship in the East, including China, where Confucianism shaped humanistic traditions.
Section A.6 covers humanities and humanistic scholarship in the East including India, where philosophical traditions shaped learning.
Sections A.2.a-e cover the relationship between theology and humanistic learning, including faith and reason and the Scholastic method.
Hermeneutics addresses the interpretation of texts, central to humanistic scholarship from Hellenistic literary criticism (A.1.e) through modern methods.
Critical theory is a major approach within the humanities (B.1-3) that engages with the question of humanities as a field of knowledge.
Postmodernism addresses the question of the humanities as a unified field of study (B.2) and challenges traditional humanistic assumptions.
Poststructuralism is a theoretical approach within the humanities that challenges traditional humanistic scholarship and its assumptions about knowledge.
Modernism represents a major movement in the arts and humanities that transformed humanistic expression and scholarship in the 20th century.
Comparative literature is a humanistic discipline that exemplifies the growth of special disciplines (A.3.h) within the humanities.
Art history is a humanistic discipline connected to the development of humanistic scholarship from the Renaissance through modern methods.
Art theory addresses the philosophical and critical dimensions of the humanities and artistic expression.
Classical literature is foundational to the humanistic tradition from Hellenistic literary criticism (A.1.e) through the quarrel of ancients and moderns (A.3.e).
English literature represents a major humanistic discipline within the growth of special disciplines (A.3.h).
Reading connects to the humanities as an educational program (B.2) and the practice of humanistic engagement with texts.
Writing is fundamental to humanistic scholarship, connected to the development of literary and textual criticism (A.1.e).
Section A.4 covers the Jewish tradition of humanistic scholarship, its nature, methods, and relation to classical and Christian learning.
Biblical studies connect to the Jewish (A.4) and Christian (A.2) traditions of humanistic scholarship and textual criticism.
Machiavelli exemplifies Renaissance humanism (A.3.b) and the development of political thought within the humanistic tradition.
Montaigne's essays exemplify Renaissance humanism (A.3.a-b) and the development of humanistic forms of philosophical and literary expression.
Goethe represents the ideal of Bildung and humanistic learning, embodying the development of humane letters (A.3).
Dante stands at the transition from medieval to Renaissance learning (A.2 to A.3), bridging Scholastic method and humanistic ideals.
The Presocratics represent the very beginnings of learning (A.1) from which the humanistic tradition emerged.
Late Antiquity covers the transition from pagan classical learning to Christian scholarship (A.2.a), a pivotal period for humanistic scholarship.
Existentialism is a 20th-century philosophical movement within the humanities concerned with human existence and meaning.
Continental philosophy represents a major tradition within the humanities engaging with hermeneutics, phenomenology, and critical theory.
Aesthetics is a humanistic discipline connecting philosophy, art, and literature, relevant to the humanities as a field of study (B.1-3).
